attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of increasing pain and swelling in his left knee. He can no longer bear weight on the affected leg. He participated in a cycling race five days ago but does not recall injuring his knee. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He drinks two glasses of wine daily.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There is a large, tender, and warm effusion around the left knee with a limited range of movement. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is highly suggestive of septic arthritis. The patient presents with an atraumatic onset of joint pain, associated with tenderness, swelling, and limited range of motion. Importantly, he also has systemic symptoms including fever (38.5°C) and tachycardia (110/min), which are characteristic of septic arthritis. These features, combined with the acute onset over two days, make septic arthritis the most likely diagnosis.

Intra-articular injury typically occurs following direct trauma or twisting mechanism. While the patient participated in a cycling race, he denies any injury. Furthermore, traumatic injuries don't typically cause significant systemic symptoms such as fever and tachycardia.

Reactive arthritis can present with acute monoarthritis following gastrointestinal or genitourinary infection. However, the onset is usually more gradual, occurring 1-4 weeks after the triggering infection. There is no history of preceding infection in this case, and the systemic symptoms are more severe than typically seen in reactive arthritis.

Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone that can present with fever and local symptoms. However, osteomyelitis typically causes more focal bone pain rather than a joint effusion, and the onset is often more gradual unless it's due to direct inoculation from trauma, which isn't present here.

Pre-patellar bursitis involves inflammation of the bursa in front of the patella. While it can cause knee pain and swelling, it typically presents as a localized swelling over the patella rather than a generalized knee effusion. It also wouldn't typically cause such significant systemic symptoms.

Septic arthritis is a medical emergency that requires prompt diagnosis and treatment to prevent joint destruction. The next steps in management would likely include joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis, blood cultures, and empiric antibiotic therapy.
learningPoints: Atraumatic onset of joint pain, associated with tenderness, swelling, and fever is most likely to be the result of septic arthritis.
Septic arthritis is a medical emergency that requires prompt diagnosis and treatment to prevent joint destruction.
Systemic symptoms such as fever and tachycardia are common in septic arthritis, distinguishing it from other causes of acute monoarthritis.
references: Horowitz DL, Katzap E, Horowitz S, et al. Approach to Septic Arthritis. Am Fam Physician 2011; 84:653-60. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2011/0915/p653.html
